A minireview on the synthesis of single atom catalysts
======================================================

Jiawen Guo a, Huimin Liu [![Image 5: ORCID logo](https://pubs.rsc.org/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8258-8263 "Select to open ORCID record for Huimin Liu (orcid.org/0000-0001-8258-8263) in a new window")*a, Dezheng Li a, Jian Wang a, Xavier Djitcheu a, Dehua He [![Image 6: ORCID logo](https://pubs.rsc.org/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0027-7002 "Select to open ORCID record for Dehua He (orcid.org/0000-0003-0027-7002) in a new window")b and Qijian Zhang [![Image 7: ORCID logo](https://pubs.rsc.org/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1992-031X "Select to open ORCID record for Qijian Zhang (orcid.org/0000-0003-1992-031X) in a new window")*a

* * *

Abstract
--------

Single atom catalysis is a prosperous and rapidly growing research field, owing to the remarkable advantages of single atom catalysts (SACs), such as maximized atom utilization efficiency, tailorable catalytic activities as well as supremely high catalytic selectivity. Synthesis approaches play crucial roles in determining the properties and performance of SACs. Over the past few years, versatile methods have been adopted to synthesize SACs. Herein, we give a thorough and up-to-date review on the progress of approaches for the synthesis of SACs, outline the general principles and list the advantages and disadvantages of each synthesis approach, with the aim to give the readers a clear picture and inspire more studies to exploit novel approaches to synthesize SACs effectively.

* * *

1. Introduction
---------------

Single atom catalysis describes a process in which a single atom on a catalyst surface drives a catalytic reaction.[1,2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit1 "Select to navigate to reference") The catalyst with a single atom on its surface is called a single atom catalyst (SAC). The concept of SAC was raised up by Zhang et al. in 2011, based on a study that a catalyst with Pt atoms singly dispersed on FeO x (named as Pt 1/FeO x) was much superior in CO oxidation reaction than its nanoparticle counterpart.[3](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit3 "Select to navigate to references") The individually dispersed active sites on SACs can ① tune electronic states, which directly or indirectly regulates the catalytic activity, and ② minimize the number of binding configuration choices between reactants and SACs, which consequently leads to an extremely high selectivity towards the target product.[4–9](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit4 "Select to navigate to reference")
Initiated by this seminal work, the nomenclature of SACs (M 1/Support) has become widely accepted, which sparked researchers' interest and boosted extensive studies.[10–20](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit10 "Select to navigate to reference") It has been proved that synthesis approaches play crucial roles in determining the properties of SACs, such as crystallinity, composition, morphology, pore structure and valence states, which eventually contributes to the performance of SACs.

Over the past few years, significant efforts have been devoted to the synthesis of SACs. To the best of our knowledge, tens of methods have been adopted to synthesize SACs. In this review, we summarize the scientific achievements of synthesis approaches of SACs to the catalysis community, aiming at providing a clear picture and inspiring more studies to exploit novel and effective approaches to synthesize SACs.

2. Synthesis approaches of SACs
-------------------------------

In this section, we divide the synthesis approaches into dry chemistry route and wet chemistry route ([Scheme 1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgsch1 "Select to navigate to scheme")), review the general principles of the approaches to synthesize SACs and compare the advantages and disadvantages of each synthesis approach.

[![Image 12: image file: d2ra00657j-s1.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-s1.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-s1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Scheme 1**Classification of the synthesis approaches of SACs.

### 2.1 Dry chemistry routes

2.1.1 Atomic layer deposition (ALD) method. ALD method also referred as atomic layer epitaxy. It was originally proposed by Finnish scientists in 1970s and developed for the preparation of polycrystalline fluorescent materials ZnS![Image 13: [thin space (1/6-em)]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_2009.gif):![Image 14: [thin space (1/6-em)]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_2009.gif)Mn[21–28](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit21 "Select to navigate to reference") and amorphous Al 2 O 3 insulating film,[29–31](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit29 "Select to navigate to reference") and recently it has been successfully used for the preparation of SACs.
An ALD reactor is indispensable for the synthesis of SACs via ALD method. The schematic illustration of a typical ALD reactor is displayed in [Fig. 1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure"). Its core is a chamber consisting of a static or rotary bed of substrates. The precursor vapor is pulsed into the chamber, where it diffuses and permeates to reach and coat the substrate surfaces.

[![Image 15: image file: d2ra00657j-f1.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f1.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 1**Schematic illustration of an ALD reactor. This figure has been adapted/reproduced from ref. [32](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit32 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from American Chemical Society, copyright 2021.

The most common mechanism for synthesizing SACs via ALD method is the ligand exchange reactions, in which the ligands or functional groups on the substrate react with the precursor. When ALD method is used to synthesize SACs, catalyst support is the substrate placed in the chamber, into which the pulse stream of precursor containing the atomically dispersed active component diffuses and reacts with the ligands or functional groups on support surface (the schematic illustration is displayed in [Fig. 2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")).[33–40](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit33 "Select to navigate to reference") The stream of precursor is stopped when the reaction between the precursor and the substrate completes. The residuals of the precursor vapor and byproducts are purged with an inert gas or evacuated at high vacuum. These two steps are called the first half-reaction. After the first half-reaction, one ALD cycle is finished. If multiple ALD cycles are needed, a second stream of precursor is pulsed into the chamber to regenerate the ligands or functional groups on support surface. After purging to remove the residuals and byproducts, it is ready for next ALD cycle (the second half-reaction in [Fig. 2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")).

[![Image 16: image file: d2ra00657j-f2.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f2.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f2_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 2**Schematic illustration of the preparation of SACs by dosing two distinct precursors sequentially to the matrix. This figure has been adapted/reproduced from ref. [32](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit32 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from American Chemical Society, copyright 2021.

According to the mechanism for the synthesis of SACs via ALD method, ligands or functional groups on support are necessary. Carbon, especially graphene, has been proved as excellent candidate support for anchoring metal atoms separately due to its unique electronic and structural properties, which facilitate the bonding between the singly dispersed atoms and carbon support through Metal–C and Metal–O–C coordination.[41](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit41 "Select to navigate to references") Using the ALD method, a number of graphene supported SACs, such as Pt 1/graphene, Pd 1/graphene and Co 1/graphene, have been successfully prepared.[42–48](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit42 "Select to navigate to reference")

Here the synthesis of Co 1/SiO 2 is taken as an example.[36](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit36 "Select to navigate to references") Gorte et al. utilized tris (2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-3,5-heptanedionato) cobalt(III) as ALD precursor, 0.7 g SiO 2 were evacuated and exposed to 5 Torr of precursor vapor at 250 °C for 5 min, after removing the excessive precursors via evacuation and removing the ligands via oxidizing it in a muffle furnace at 500 °C for 7 min, one ALD cycle is finished and Co 1/SiO 2 is obtained. The number of singly dispersed atoms on SACs could be controlled by adjusting the number of ALD cycles. Gorte et al. further investigated the effects of the number of ALD cycles on the properties of the as-prepared catalysts. They discovered that isolated Co catalyst could be obtained with only one ALD cycle. Co loadings increased linearly with the number of ALD cycles. After several cycles, the single dispersion state of Co remained. Further increasing the number of ALD cycles, Co gradually transformed from the single dispersion state to clusters and eventually to nanoparticles.[36](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit36 "Select to navigate to references")

ALD possesses the following advantages, (1) the deposition parameters could be controlled precisely, (2) the desired thickness or size of the deposited precursor could be achieved by adjusting the number of cycles, (3) the deposition uniformity and reproducibility are excellent in spite that slight deviations from the ideal conditions are sometimes observed, and (4) different deposition materials can be controlled precisely at the atomic level to form composites with various morphologies.[31,49–54](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit31 "Select to navigate to reference") The advantages of ALD make it possible for providing ideal model catalysts for the basic studies of catalysis, which include exploring the effects of particle sizes, surface properties of catalyst supports and encapsulation (or surface coating) of metal or alloy nanoparticles, on the catalytic performance. In conclusion, ALD is an important approach to synthesize SACs and study the structure–activity relationship over SACs. The drawback is that, only materials with suitable ligands or functional groups could be chosen as support to disperse the metal atoms separately via ALD method, meanwhile, this technology is not applicable for the commercial preparation of SACs currently, because of the stability and cost issues ([Table 1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#tab1 "Select to navigate to table")).

**Table 1**The metals used in the synthesis of SACs by ALD

| Method | Singly dispersed metals |
| :--- | :--- |
| ALD | Pt, Pd, Co, Cu, Fe, Ni, Ti, Zn[32](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit32 "Select to navigate to references") |

* * *

2.1.2 Pyrolysis synthesis method. Pyrolysis synthesis, as the name suggested, is a strategy to synthesize SACs by thermochemically decomposing the organic precursors of the catalysts at elevated temperatures under a specific atmosphere. A series of metal–nitrogen–carbon SACs have been successfully synthesized via this method in the past decade.[55–76](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit55 "Select to navigate to reference")
At present, the precursors for the preparation of SACs by this method are mainly divided into metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), metal phthalocyanine–silica colloid composites, metal–phenanthroline complexes on carbon supports and amino-functionalized rigid molecules. Here, several examples are elaborated to illustrate the preparation procedures for SACs via the pyrolysis of the several above-mentioned precursors. (1) For the preparation of stable Co–nitrogen–carbon SAC, bimetallic Zn/Co MOF is an excellent precursor. During the following pyrolysis process, Zn could be selectively evaporated away at high temperatures above 800 °C and Co could be reduced by the carbonization of the organic linker. The loading of singly dispersed Co atoms achieved via this method could be high up to 4 wt%.[77](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit77 "Select to navigate to references") Other metal–nitrogen–carbon SACs (M = W, Mo, Cu, Zn, Cr, Mn, Fe and Ni) could also be obtained via the pyrolysis synthesis method with bimetallic-organic frameworks as precursors.[78–83](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit78 "Select to navigate to reference") (2) When Co phthalocyanine–silica colloid composite was used as precursor, singly dispersed Co catalyst Co–N x/C could be prepared by the pyrolysis of the precursor and the subsequent removal of silica template and cobalt nanoparticles.[84](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit84 "Select to navigate to references") The as prepared Co–N x/C SAC showed extremely high activity, chemoselectivity and stability toward the reduction of nitro compounds by H 2. (3) The noble-metal-free Co–N–C catalyst could also be derived from the pyrolysis of cobalt–phenanthroline complexes on a mesoporous carbon support at high temperatures,[85](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit85 "Select to navigate to references") and this Co–N–C catalyst was reported to show high catalytic activity (turnover frequency, TOF, of 3.8 s−1 based on Co single atoms) and good recyclability in the aerobic oxidation of over 28 examples of diverse substrates. (4) The preparation of Pd 1/N-graphene from amino-functionalized rigid molecules is another example for pyrolysis synthesis method. In the preparation procedure, a precursor was firstly obtained by functionalizing the palladium phthalocyanines with four aminophenoxy groups at the periphery of the benzene ring of graphene, which was then pyrolyzed to anchor the isolated Pd atoms.[86](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit86 "Select to navigate to references")

Taking the Co–N–C catalyst derived from the pyrolysis of cobalt–phenanthroline complexes on a mesoporous carbon support and Pd 1/N-graphene prepared from amino-functionalized rigid molecules as typical examples, high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images clearly indicated that the precursors were successfully transformed into singly dispersed atoms ([Fig. 3a–d](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig3 "Select to navigate to figure")).[85,86](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit85 "Select to navigate to reference")

[![Image 17: image file: d2ra00657j-f3.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f3.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f3_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 3**HAADF-STEM image of Co–N–C catalyst prepared by the pyrolysis synthesis method (a and b) and Pd 1/N-graphene catalyst prepared by the pyrolysis synthesis method (c and d). These figures have been adapted/reproduced from ref. [85](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit85 "Select to navigate to references") and [86](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit86 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from American Chemical Society and Royal Society of Chemistry, copyright 2015 and 2022.

The pyrolysis synthesis method is a straightforward route for the preparation of SACs. The singly dispersed atoms could be derived from precursors, in which there are suitable interactions between the metal precursor and support. Generally, the singly dispersed atoms are transitional metals, such as W, Mo, Cu, Zn, Cr, Mn, Fe and Ni, while the supports are originated from MOFs, silica colloids, carbon or amino-functionalized rigid molecules. Upon pyrolysis in a furnace under a specific atmosphere, the precursors gradually decompose, lead to the transformation of precursor to the desired support and single atoms. However, high pyrolysis temperatures are generally required, which makes it an energy intensive process. In addition, up to now, there have been no reports on the synthesis of non-metal-based SACs via this method ([Table 2](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#tab2 "Select to navigate to table")).

**Table 2**Summary of precursors and metals for pyrolysis synthesis of SACs

| Method | Precursors | Single-atom metals |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pyrolysis synthesis | MOFs | W, Mo, Cu, Zn, Cr, Mn, Fe, Ni[78–83](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit78 "Select to navigate to reference") |
| Silica colloids |
| Carbon or amino-functionalized rigid molecules[77](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit77 "Select to navigate to references") |

* * *

2.1.3 Atom trapping method. Atom trapping method is a promising approach to synthesize singly dispersed catalysts by utilizing the migration property of metal atoms at elevated temperatures. It was initially adopted for the synthesis of SACs in 2016 by Jones et al., who reported that a thermally stable single atom Pt 1/CeO 2 catalyst was successfully achieved by aging a mixture of Pt/Al 2 O 3 and CeO 2 in air at 800 °C to promote the transfer of Pt from Al 2 O 3 to CeO 2, where it was strongly anchored as single site ([Fig. 4](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig4 "Select to navigate to figure")).[87](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit87 "Select to navigate to references") CeO 2 nanorods were more effective than CeO 2 cubes at trapping the Pt atoms. Aging at high temperatures ensured that only the most stable binding sites on CeO 2 were occupied, and thus yielded a sintering-resistant atomically dispersed catalyst.[87](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit87 "Select to navigate to references") The transformation of nanoparticles to stable single atoms could be directly observed via HAADF-STEM. [Fig. 5a–d](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure") displayed the evolution of Pd nanoparticles on zinc 2-methylimidazole metal organic framework (ZIF-8) to single atoms. [Fig. 5a](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure") was the HAADF-STEM image of ZIF-8. During the first stage of heating, the crystalline Pd nanoparticles became larger and the size distribution became inhomogeneous (Intermediate I, [Fig. 5b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure")). Upon heating the second stage, the number of nanoparticles was significantly reduced, and the crystalline Pd nanoparticles were transformed into an amorphous state (Intermediate II, [Fig. 5c](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure")). With the further extension of heating time, the remaining nanoparticles were fully digested within the substrate. At the same time, ZIF-8 was decomposed and converted into SACs ([Fig. 5d](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure")).[88](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit88 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 18: image file: d2ra00657j-f4.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f4.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f4_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 4**Illustration of the synthesis of Pt 1/CeO 2 SAC via the trapping of mobile Pt by CeO 2 due to the migration of Pt at high temperatures. This figure has been adapted/reproduced from ref. [87](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit87 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from American Association for the Advancement of Science, copyright 2016.

[![Image 19: image file: d2ra00657j-f5.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f5.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d2ra00657j/d2ra00657j-f5_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 5**Evolution of Pd nanoparticles on ZIF-8 to single atoms via HAADF-STEM. (a) Pd nanoparticles on ZIF-8, (b) intermediate I, (c) intermediate II and (d) Pd single atoms. This figure has been adapted/reproduced from ref. [88](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit88 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018.

The atom trapping method is applicable to synthesize a series of SACs, including Pd,[88](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit88 "Select to navigate to references") Au,[88](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit88 "Select to navigate to references") Cu[89](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit89 "Select to navigate to references") and Ni[90](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit90 "Select to navigate to references") SACs. These SACs could be synthesized by transforming the relevant nanoparticles in an inert atmosphere above 900 °C, when nitrogen-doped carbon is used as support to capture the mobile atoms.[88](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#cit88 "Select to navigate to references")

The atom trapping method is a simple approach for synthesizing SACs. Nevertheless, the approach requires a supply of mobile atoms and a support that can trap the mobile species. Additionally, the very high synthesis temperatures are not favorable from an energy perspective. However, if the catalyst preparation process is applied to high-speed moving automobile exhaust, its temperature can be easily reached under operation, which makes this method very practical ([Table 3](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d2ra00657j#tab3 "Select to navigate to table")).

The bigger picture
------------------

With the technological and scientific progress in synthesis, characterization, and surface science, as well as theoretical and computational chemistry, the development of catalysts has gradually transformed from selection done “blindly” through trial and error to research based on rational design. In recent years, single-atom catalysis and its atom-level regulation research have received enormous interest from academic research, bringing light to atomically precise catalysis. Remarkably, atom-level regulation of single-atom catalysts has a particular focus on the interatomic interfaces and interactions between active atoms, which are expected to offer a comprehensive and deep understanding of catalytic processes through exploration of the potential interatomic synergy effect. By virtue of single-atom catalysts and their atom-level regulation, rational design and controllable synthesis has been pushed to a new stage. In this review, we survey relevant progress in atomic-level regulation strategies, and we summarize the critical factors for effective regulation and improvement of single-atom catalysts.

Summary
-------

Single-atom catalysts have become the forefront and hotspot in catalysis research due to their unique structural characteristics such as extreme atomic utilization efficiency and uniformity of reaction centers. To break through the bottleneck on their intrinsic activity enhancement, arduous efforts have been made to regulate the metal single-atomic sites. Herein, with a special interest in interatomic interfaces and interactions, our review focuses on the atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts, including nonmetal [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and polymetallic active site construction, referring to the introduction of nonmetal or metal atoms, respectively, into primitive metal single-atomic sites. We summarize the progress of these two strategies in optimizing single-atom active sites from the perspectives of synthesis, application, and mechanism. We start from a systematic overview of the atomic-level regulation methods and corresponding fabrication routes, followed by a comprehensive summary of the current advances in some typical [electrocatalytic reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-reaction "Learn more about electrocatalytic reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Then we expound on the synergistic reaction mechanism and the intrinsic structure-activity relationship after regulation. In the final part, we discuss the challenges and opportunities of these two atomic-level regulation strategies for further development. Our review of the atomic-level regulation of single-atom catalysts gives significant insight into the rational design of atom-efficient catalysts and adds depth and breadth to the fundamental understanding of atomically precise catalysis by elucidating its intrinsic mechanisms.

Introduction
------------

Single-atom catalysts are a new catalytic material family composed of isolated metal single-atom and support material with distinctive structural properties,[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib2) such as isolated metal active centers, extreme dispersion, highly unsaturated coordination environment, and uniform reaction centers.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib5),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib6) By virtue of their unique structural characteristics, single-atom catalysis has been regarded as a pioneer of atomically precise catalysis. More importantly, rational design and controllable synthesis of catalysts have been pushed to a new stage along with the vigorous development in single-atom catalysis.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib7) Although the development of single-atom catalysts continued to flourish,[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib12),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib13) the catalytic activity still remains to be improved, which may be severely restricted by the density of the isolated metal active centers[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib16) and their intrinsic activity.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) In fact, arduous efforts have been dedicated to regulate the intrinsic activity, which is based on control and optimization of the energy level structure and surface electronic structure.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib18) Hence, it can improve the catalytic performance even with a limited number of active sites, which is different from increasing density by adding the number of accessible catalytic active sites.

In recent years, atomic-level regulation strategies for single-atom catalysts have received enormous interest from the academic research community. Inspired by the [heteroatom](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/heteroatom "Learn more about heteroatom from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") doping modification, a common strategy is to introduce nonmetal heteroatoms with different [electronegativity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electronegativity "Learn more about electronegativity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from the atoms in the original support, such as N, S, P, O, B, etc.,[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib19),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib20) called nonmetal [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strategy. In fact, the active center of single-atom catalysts usually refers to the collective entity of the central metal single atom and the coordination atoms from the support, in which the coordination atoms exert unprecedented influence on the formation, stability, and overall catalytic performance of single-atom metal active centers,[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib3),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib21) and become a more promising point for catalysts modification. Nonmetal heteroatom doping strategy introduces nonmetal heteroatoms so as to change the type or number of coordination atoms, which can achieve a significant change on the [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") state, electronic structure, and local spatial configuration of the central metal atom.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) Furthermore, doped nonmetal heteroatoms may participate in the process of adsorption for reaction molecules and assist the formation of reaction intermediates, so as to adjust and enhance the catalytic performance.

In addition, another atomic-level regulation strategy is called construction of polymetallic active sites, derived from the adjustment mechanism of metal alloy structure.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) It introduces other atomic metal site into the single-atom catalysts for catalytic performance enhancement. To some extent, construction of polymetallic active sites can be regarded as metal atom doping, and is expected to further improve the activity and selectivity by their unique multi-metal active site structure. Up to now, the dual-atom catalysts (DACs) constructed by introducing only another one atomic metal site are a typical representative among them.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib22) By formation of a homonuclear or heteronuclear dual-atom metal site, it might be possible to modulate the catalytic performance through various interatomic interactions (such as bifunctional effects, electronic structure reconstruction, and geometric configuration regulation).[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib23)

Atomic-level regulation strategies, including nonmetal heteroatom doping and polymetallic active site construction, are of great importance not only for fundamental research but also for practical application. (1) Firstly, atomic-level regulation based on defined active centers from single-atom catalysts constructs the new active centers with more precise atomic numbers and configurations than other methods. Consequently, it becomes a viable way to provides ideal models for structure-property investigation, bringing light to atomically precise catalysis.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib24) (2) Secondly, it has a particular focus on the interatomic interfaces and interactions between active atoms, which are expected to acquire a comprehensive and deep understanding of the catalytic processes by exploring the [potential interatomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/interatomic-potential "Learn more about potential interatomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") synergy effect. (3) Thirdly, it has been demonstrated to effectively enhance the catalytic activity and selectivity in many Herein, this review predominantly aims at elucidating the atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts, by which we are referring to atomic modification on the primitive metal single-atomic site by introducing nonmetal or metal atoms, respectively (shown as [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#fig1)). More specifically, we start from a systematic overview on their fabrication routes and methods, and next summarize their current advances applied in some typical [electrocatalytic reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-reaction "Learn more about electrocatalytic reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as ORR, OER, HER, CO 2 RR, and so on. Then, we expound the synergistic reaction mechanism and the intrinsic structure–activity relationship after regulation. At the end of this review, we present the challenges and opportunities of these two atomic-level regulation strategies. Along with ceaseless innovation and maturation of its technology, atomic-level regulation for single-atom catalysts would significantly facilitate the rational design of high-efficiency nanocatalysts, and sharpens our understanding of atomically precise catalysis by elucidating the interatomic synergy mechanisms.

Nonmetal heteroatom doping
--------------------------

Nonmetal [heteroatom](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/heteroatom "Learn more about heteroatom from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") doping strategy is a typical strategy in the atomic-level regulation for single-atom catalysts, especially for carbon-based single-atom catalysts. [Carbon material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-material "Learn more about Carbon material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a kind of environmental-friendly material with high performance,[70](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib70) for its specific features such as high specific [surface area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-area "Learn more about surface area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), superb chemical and mechanical dependability, adjustable morphological and structural combinations, tunable porosity and surface properties, and good electrical and [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib71) Hence, carbon-based material can achieve different functions by ingenious design and precision synthesis for [chemical structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/structure-composition "Learn more about chemical structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is commonly used as metal-free catalysts or as a support in single-atom catalysts, and widely applied and researched in the field of catalysis, energy, ecology, and so on.[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib71),[72](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib72),[73](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib73) However, with the primitive carbon material there is difficulty in providing strong metal-support interaction to anchor isolate metal atoms,[74](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib74) making unsuitable to be directly used as a support material for preparation of single-atom catalysts.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) Defects or heteroatom sites act as anchor sites for isolated metal atoms through strong metal-support interaction. The construction of carbon defect sites to coordinate with metal atoms is a relatively complex process, while it is easy to implement to dope nonmetallic heteroatoms in carbon materials.

Compared with the primitive carbon material, [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can effectively regulate the performance of the material and provide more suitable anchoring sites for the isolated metal atom, and play a role in regulating the [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") state and electronic structure of the central metal atom. The N element has high similarity and compatibility with the C element, so it has been the most deeply studied doped element. [N doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/n-type-doping "Learn more about N doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cannot only increase the conductivity of carbon materials, but also improve the activity of carbon-based materials.[75](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib75) Recently, many studies have been carried out on the structure of multiple heteroatoms. These works are mostly carried out on the basis of N atom doping, introducing a second kind of heteroatom, such as S,[76](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib76) P,[77](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib77) O,[78](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib78) B,[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib79) etc. By participating in central metal-support coordination, heteroatom doping is able to reduce the high surface energy of the isolated metal atom, which not only stabilizes the highly dispersed metal single-atom active sites but also modulates the activity. It can also be used as an "environment atom" to replace the original carbon atom in the skeleton, and there is a long-range interaction between the support and the metal single-atom active site, effectively improving the catalytic activity of the catalyst. For example, Xie’s group reported a strategy of sulfur doping (S doping) to achieve effective regulation of the [electrocatalytic activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-activity "Learn more about electrocatalytic activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the Fe–NC active site.[80](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib80) They have demonstrated atomic dispersion of Fe–N _x_ species on N and S codecorated hierarchical carbon layers and employed the material as a [bifunctional catalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/bifunctional-catalyst "Learn more about bifunctional catalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The improvement of the electrocatalytic performance of Fe–N–C active centers after S atom doping was attributed to the uneven distribution of charge caused by doping and the increase of Fe 3d electron conductivity by sulfur substitution.

# Large-scale synthesis of functional single-atom catalysts

[*Communications Chemistry*](/commschem)
**volume 6**, Article number: 36 (2023)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

2571 Accesses

5 Citations

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4/metrics)

Economical and high-efficiency synthesis of single-atom catalysts is a tremendous challenge hampering their large-scale industrialization, which is mainly attributed to the complex equipment and processes necessary for both top-down and bottom-up synthesis methods. Now, a facile three-dimensional printing approach tackles this dilemma. From a solution of printing ink and metal precursors, target materials with specific geometric shapes are prepared with high output, directly and automatically.

Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have become an emerging class of materials in the field of catalysis due to their 100% atomic utilization, controllable coordination environment, quantum size effects, unique electron-geometry structure and, ultimately, their excellent catalytic performance[1](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR1 "Zhang, W. & Zheng, W. Single atom excels as the smallest functional material. Adv. Funct. Mater. 26, 2988–2993 (2016)."). However, both the process of dimensionality reduction from bulk materials to single atoms, as well as the process of synthesis of host materials with subsequent high-temperature pyrolysis to prepare single atoms, involve complex wet-chemical steps. Dynamic assembly processes of SACs vary with experimental parameters; notorious sintering steps can lead to the formation of clusters and/or SACs. Similar synthetic challenges also exist for zero-dimensional quantum dot materials[2](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR2 "Liu, Y., Zhang, W. & Zheng, W. Surface chemistry of MXene quantum dots: Virus mechanism-inspired mini-lab for catalysis. Chinese. J. Catal. 43, 2913–2935 (2022)."),[3](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR3 "Liu, Y. et al. MXene-based quantum dots optimize hydrogen production via spontaneous evolution of Cl- to O-terminated Surface Groups. Energy Environ. Mater. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/eem2.12438
                  
                 (2022)."). To increase production volume and effective loadings of SACs, efforts have been concentrated on preparing SACs precursors, such as by regulating ligand environment and construction of substrate defects[4](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR4 "Tan, W. et al. Fine-tuned local coordination environment of Pt single atoms on ceria controls catalytic reactivity. Nat. Commun. 13, 7070 (2022)."),[5](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR5 "Yang, Q. et al. Single carbon vacancy traps atomic platinum for hydrogen evolution catalysis. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 144, 2171–2178 (2022)."). Some improved mechanical methods have been applied to replace complicated wet-chemistry processes, such as mechanochemical abrasion, high-temperature-shockwave and laser-irradiation syntheses[6](#ref-CR6 "Yao, Y. et al. High temperature shockwave stabilized single atoms. Nat. Nanotechnol. 14, 851–857 (2019)."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Han, G.-F. et al. Abrading bulk metal into single atoms. Nat. Nanotechnol. 17, 403–407 (2022)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "He, X. et al. Mechanochemical kilogram-scale synthesis of noble metal single-atom catalysts. Cell Rep. Phys. Sci. 1, 100004 (2020)."),[9](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR9 "Khan, K. et al. Laser-irradiated holey graphene-supported single-atom catalyst towards hydrogen evolution and oxygen reduction. Adv. Energy Mater. 11, 2101619 (2021)."). Various strategies to improve the loadings of SACs, such as defect engineering, coordinated design, and space restriction, have been reported[10](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR10 "Jing, T., Li, T., Rao, D., Wang, M. & Zuo, Y. Defining the loading of single-atom catalysts: weight fraction or atomic fraction? Mater Today Energy. 31, 101197 (2023)."). While these methods have greatly improved the yield of SACs syntheses—marking a big step towards the goal of promoting SACs from laboratory to industrial production—cost-effectiveness, green chemistry, and convenience remain key factors of concern.

Now, Prof. Shi-Zhang Qiao from the University of Adelaide, Australia, together with an international team of researchers, reported a universal three-dimensional (3D) printing approach for preparing a library of SACs (<https://doi.org/10.1038/s44160-022-00193-3>)[11](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR11 "Xie, F. et al. A general approach to 3D-printed single-atom catalysts. Nat. Synth. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s44160-022-00193-3
                  
                 (2023)."). “3D printing allows the customization of geometric designs from millimeters to meters, which is vital for industrial applications,” says Prof. Qiao. “The combination of 3D printing and single-atom catalysts provides a promising but simplified way to manufacture SACs at different scales.” The strategy is realized by mixing natural polymer printing inks and metal precursor solutions to produce centimeter-sized SACs precursor materials directly. The natural polymers are then carbonized, and SACs are simultaneously obtained via this heat treatment step (Fig. [1A](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#Fig1)). Thereby, the metal atoms are dissolved and undergo dynamic evolution processes[12](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#ref-CR12 "Zhang, L. et al. Direct visualization of the evolution of a single-atomic cobalt catalyst from melting nanoparticles with carbon dissolution. Adv. Sci. 9, 2200592 (2022)."), including liquid metal agglomeration, dispersion, re-aggregation and sublimation, and then, captured by the carbonized carrier, a single-atom coordination structure is formed. The metallic atoms are confirmed as being atomically dispersed on the carbon substrate, as shown by the group through high-resolution high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) characterizations (Fig. [1B, C](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#Fig1)). Central atom type (M), coordination environment, as well as loading of the catalyst, can be varied. Qiao and team have selected different transition metal phthalocyanine and acetylacetone as precursors to prepare SACs with M–N–C and M–O–C coordinations, respectively. The M–O coordination can also be changed to a M–O–Cl coordination through subsequent hydrochloric acid treatment (Fig. [1D](/articles/s42004-023-00834-4#Fig1)). By expanding or reducing the volume of the metal precursor, the loadings of single-atom sites can easily be regulated. This confirms the method’s versatility and offers an opportunity for SACs to meet the needs of a wide range of industrial applications. The synthesized catalysts are used for the reduction reaction of nitrate, attesting to the great potential of the manufacturing method in the field of electrocatalysis.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42004-023-00834-4/MediaObjects/42004_2023_834_Fig1_HTML.png)

This study illustrates the versatility and convenience of 3D printing and the technique’s suitability to reduce the cost of large-scale manufacturing of SACs. “3D-printed SACs pave the way to a scalable and sustainable production of valuable fuels and chemicals,” says Qiao. It is of great guiding significance to explore the structure–function relationship at the atomic level and gain mechanistic insight into functional materials. We hope the team’s efforts encourage the community to look closely at 3D-printed mono- and bimetallic SACs towards greater selectivity for catalytic reactions. One could envision this study as the starting point for a universal approach for a given material to be transformed into a pool of functionalized single atoms towards an even more comprehensive range of advanced functional materials.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Top-down methods involve breaking down bulk materials into single atoms, while bottom-up approaches synthesize single atoms from precursors, enhancing control over structure and properties.

Mechanical processes utilize physical methods like grinding or milling, whereas chemical processes involve reactions that yield single atoms through techniques such as pyrolysis and sol-gel synthesis.
3D printing facilitates the scalable and customizable synthesis of single-atom catalysts, allowing precise control over geometry and composition while reducing costs and complexity.
